results.title: Reinforcement Learning vs Agentic AI: Two Different Meanings
results.content:
Understanding ‘Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards’ (RLVR) As AI moves from experimental to enterprise-ready, one of the persistent challenges is how to train models reliably, transparently and for business outcomes. Traditional reinforcement-learning setups often rely on human labels, proxy metrics or learned reward models — all of which introduce ambiguity and risk. RLVR offers an alternative: rule-based feedback in domains where correctness can be verified (think math, code, certain decision-tasks) and a binary reward signal (e.g., correct/incorrect). Why should marketing & business executives care? Because: - It improves model trustworthiness and governance. - It lowers the barrier between proof-of-concept and production-scale deployment. - It can sharpen alignment [...] The word “reinforcement” shows up in both Reinforcement Learning and Agentic AI — but it means two very different things. In Reinforcement Learning (RL), reinforcement is literal. An agent interacts with its environment, gets rewards or penalties, and adjusts its behavior. Just like training a dog with treats, the feedback loop reinforces the right actions over time. In Agentic AI, reinforcement is metaphorical. Here, it refers to reinforced capabilities — reasoning, planning, memory, and tool use. Instead of learning from numeric rewards, the system is “reinforced” with extra abilities that let it act autonomously, like a smart assistant with access to calendars, emails, and apps. 👉 RL = reinforcement through feedback signals. 👉 Agentic AI = reinforcement through augmented intelligence [...] 18   2 Comments

Like   Comment

Ashokekumar Selvamani   3mo 

 Report this comment

Nicely put 👏 — RL and Agentic AI really do use ‘reinforcement’ in different ways. RL strengthens behavior through feedback loops (rewards/penalties), while Agentic AI strengthens autonomy with added capabilities like reasoning, planning, memory, and tool use. What’s fascinating is how they can complement each other: agentic systems can use RL to refine their strategies over time, while RL agents become far more practical when enhanced with agentic abilities like long-term memory or external tools. Different mechanics, but both are pushing AI toward greater adaptability and autonomy.

Like Reply  2 Reactions   3 Reactions

Well worded! Rajesh Krishnan

results.title: LiteResearcher: A Scalable Agentic RL Training Framework for Deep Research Agent
results.content:
Reinforcement Learning (RL) has emerged as a powerful training paradigm for LLM-based agents. However, scaling agentic RL for deep research remains constrained by two coupled challenges: hand-crafted synthetic data fails to elicit genuine real-world search capabilities, and real-world search dependency during RL training introduces instability and prohibitive cost, which limits the scalability of Agentic RL. LiteResearcher is a training framework that makes Agentic RL scalable: by constructing a lite virtual world that mirrors real-world search dynamics, we enable a continuously improving training recipe that empowers a tiny search agent to outperform large-scale open-source and commercial models (e.g., Tongyi DeepResearch and Claude-4.5 Sonnet). Specifically, on common benchmarks such as [...] #### Information Source Masking

To prevent the agent from relying on trivial shortcuts, we implement a source masking mechanism. After a QA pair is generated, we deliberately delete its original information source from the local corpus. This forces the agent to navigate the expanded corpus to discover alternative, non-trivial search pathways, naturally eliciting the complex atomic search capabilities detailed in Table 1.

#### Strict Quality and Difficulty Filtering [...] In this paper, we presented LiteResearcher, an agentic RL training framework that resolves the critical bottlenecks of data diversity and environment stability by constructing an isolated virtual world. Crucially, our work yields three key insights for the future of deep research agents. First, we demonstrate that Agentic RL can drive sustained capability improvement. The training saturation observed in prior works is due to reliance on high-variance live environments or a narrow local corpus, rather than a limitation of the RL paradigm itself. Second, we reveal that a simple but scalable synthetic data pipeline, driven by the continuous expansion of real-world information sources, is significantly more effective than over-engineered, handcrafted reasoning structures. Finally, trained

results.title: The Landscape of Agentic Reinforcement Learning for LLMs: A Survey
results.content:
Abstract:The emergence of agentic reinforcement learning (Agentic RL) marks a paradigm shift from conventional reinforcement learning applied to large language models (LLM RL), reframing LLMs from passive sequence generators into autonomous, decision-making agents embedded in complex, dynamic worlds. This survey formalizes this conceptual shift by contrasting the degenerate single-step Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) of LLM RL with the temporally extended Partially Observable Markov Decision Processes (POMDPs) that define Agentic RL. Building on this foundation, we propose a comprehensive twofold taxonomy: one organized around core agentic capabilities, including planning, tool use, memory, reasoning, self-improvement, and perception, and the other around their applications across [...] 1.   Theoretical Formalization: Establishes a rigorous distinction between conventional LLM-RL (T=1) and Agentic RL (T>1), providing clear definitions of state spaces, action spaces, and learning objectives.
2.   Dual Taxonomy Framework: Introduces classification system examining (a) core agentic capabilities where RL serves as the optimization mechanism (planning, tool use, memory, reasoning, self-improvement), and (b) practical applications across domains (search, code generation, vision, embodied agents, multi-agent systems).
3.   Resource Collection: Consolidates environments, frameworks, and benchmarks into an actionable reference.

results.title: What is Agentic Reinforcement Learning - WTF In Tech - Substack
results.content:
Traditional reinforcement learning, especially in language models, often works in a single-step setup. The model receives an input, generates an output, and gets a reward. That’s it. There is no ongoing interaction or evolving state. Agentic RL, on the other hand, introduces multi-step decision-making. The agent interacts with an environment, updates its understanding, and continuously refines its strategy. This shift allows AI to move beyond static responses into dynamic problem-solving.

#### Key Differences [...] Agentic Reinforcement Learning is an advanced form of reinforcement learning where an AI system behaves like an agent,it takes actions, observes outcomes, and adjusts its behavior over time to achieve a goal. Unlike traditional systems that generate a single response, agentic systems operate in loops. They plan multiple steps, interact with tools, and adapt based on feedback. In simple terms, the AI is no longer just answering,it is doing. [...] Scenario: A regional trade disruption blocks a primary shipping route.
 Agent Action: The system automatically identifies alternative suppliers via API, recalculates the “Total Cost of Ownership,” and re-routes shipments.
 The RL Edge: Agents are rewarded for balancing speed vs. cost, allowing them to navigate geopolitical volatility better than static scripts.

### 3. “Adaptive Middleware” in Education

The 2026 shift in EdTech is moving from “Content” to “Outcomes.”

 Pattern: Agents act as a “Predictive Safety Net,” identifying students likely to drop out 3–4 weeks before they actually do.
 Action: The agent dynamically pivots the curriculum—if a student struggles with a theory, it generates a practical case study in an industry they like (e.g., Renewable Energy).

results.title: Open Source RL Libraries for LLMs | Anyscale
results.content:
OpenRLHF: OpenRLHF was built for RLHF and has very good support for reward models and different optimization algorithms. It also supports asynchronous training as well as colocation of generation and training. There is some support for environments / agentic RL but no dedicated interfaces. OpenRLHF is mature and has a sizable community, and there are libraries built on top of it that extend it more towards reasoning as well as multi-agent training domains.

RAGEN: RAGEN is a library built on top of Verl which integrates a more explicit environment interface, and extends verl with better agent and multi-turn RL support. It also makes it easier to define custom environments. [...] SkyRL: SkyRL incorporates learnings from many of the above libraries and provides simple yet flexible interfaces designed to support a broad range of settings, built with multi-turn agentic settings in mind. For example, it provides support for: sync or async one-off pipelining, colocated or disaggregated generation or training, external or integrated inference engine, and different methods for weight synchronization. SkyRL was recently released and is not as mature yet and has less of an ecosystem, but provides several example scripts for agentic scenarios. [...] Agentic and multi-step RL: In this setting, the LLM operates as an autonomous agent within an environment, executing multiple sequential actions to accomplish a task. This represents the most sophisticated configuration and places the highest demands on the supporting library. The library must coordinate both the environment and the LLM, while handling problems that may require very different numbers of steps across various instances.
